CAT3D creates a 3D representation of the scene in as little as one minute. CAT3D has two stages: (1) generate a large set of synthetic views with a multi-view latent diffusion model conditioned on the input views and target poses; (2) run a robust 3D reconstruction pipeline on the observed and generated views.
We train a multi-view diffusion model that takes a single or multiple views of a 3D scene as input and generates multiple output images given their camera poses
run a robust 3D reconstruction pipeline on the observed and generated views

Specifically, given M conditional views containing M images Icond and their corresponding camera parameters pcond, the model learns to capture the joint distribution of N target images Itgt assuming their N target camera parameters ptgt are also given
the model learns to capture the joint distribution of N target images

The model encodes every individual image into a latent representation through an image variational auto-encoder.
A diffusion model is trained to estimate the joint distribution of the latent representations given conditioning signals.
We initialize the model from an LDM trained for text-to-image generation similar to [62] trained on web-scale image data.
As is often done in video diffusion models, the main backbone of our model remains the pretrained 2D diffusion model.
We directly inflate the existing 2D self-attention layers after every 2D residual block of the original LDM to connect latents with 3D self-attention layers.
Conditioning on input views through 3D self-attention layers removed the need for PixelNeRF and CLIP image embeddings used by the prior state-of-the-art model on few-view reconstruction, ReconFusion.
We use FlashAttention for fast training and sampling.

The weights of the latent diffusion model are similar to those of prior work in terms of shifting the noise schedule towards high noise levels as we move from a pre-trained image diffusion model to a multi-view diffusion model.

The weights of the latent diffusion model are shifted towards high noise levels to capture data of higher dimensionality, similar to the shift observed from a pre-trained image diffusion model to a multi-view diffusion model.
The noise schedule is shifted from the pre-trained image diffusion model to the multi-view diffusion model to move towards high noise levels to handle data of higher dimensionality.
The log signal-to-noise ratio is shifted by log(N) towards the high signal-to-noise ratio region, where N is the number of target views, similar to the logic followed by prior work.
The latents of target images are noise perturbed while those of conditional images are clean, and the diffusion loss is defined only on target images.
A binary mask is concatenated to the latents along the channel dimension to denote conditioning vs. target images during training.
To condition on the camera pose, a camera ray representation ('raymap') is used. It encodes the ray origin and direction at each spatial location relative to the camera pose.
Rays are computed relative to the camera pose of the first conditional image, making the pose representation invariant to rigid transformations of 3D world coordinates.
Raymaps for each image are concatenated channel-wise onto the latents for the corresponding image, indicating a relationship between camera pose and raymaps.

The goal of generating a large set of consistent views from a set of input views is dependent on the camera pose, as it influences the decision on the set of camera poses to sample and the design of a sampling strategy.
In 3D scene reconstruction, designing reasonable camera trajectories is crucial, different from orbital camera trajectories used in 3D object reconstruction.
The challenge in 3D scene reconstruction is designing camera trajectories that fully cover the scene content, which is different from the approach in 3D object reconstruction.
Designing camera paths is crucial for achieving compelling few-view 3D reconstruction, as the paths must be thorough and dense to fully-constrain the reconstruction problem.
Camera paths must not pass through objects in the scene or view scene content from unusual angles to ensure accurate 3D reconstruction.
Different types of camera paths are explored based on the characteristic of the scene, including orbital paths, circle paths, spline paths, and spiral trajectories.
The orbital paths involve moving around the center scene from different scales and heights.
Circle paths move forward in the scene from different scales and offsets.
Spline paths follow different offsets in the scene.
Spiral trajectories move along a cylindrical path, entering and exiting the scene.
The challenge of applying the multi-view diffusion model to novel view synthesis is addressed by clustering target viewpoints into smaller groups and generating them independently.
The multi-view diffusion model was trained with a small and finite set of 8 input and output views.
Target views are grouped based on close camera positions, as these views are typically the most dependent.
An autoregressive sampling strategy is adopted for single-image conditioning, starting with generating a set of 7 anchor views that cover the scene.
The anchor views are used in parallel to generate the remaining groups of views, ensuring long-range consistency between anchor views and local similarity between nearby views.
There exists a consistency between anchor views and local similarity between nearby views.
In the single-image setting, 80 views are generated, and in the few-view setting, 480-960 views are used.

The video diffusion architecture utilizes a per-image embedding of the camera pose to improve samples and 3D reconstructions, while also reducing model complexity and the number of parameters.
Embedding the camera pose as a low-dimensional vector works well for in-domain samples but generalizes poorly compared to raymap conditioning.
Raymap conditioning generalizes better compared to embedding the camera pose as a low-dimensional vector when dealing with out-of-domain samples.
Modeling multiple output views (e.g., 5 or 7 views instead of 1) has been found to improve sample metrics and the quality of each output image independently.
Utilizing 3D self-attention has shown to yield improved performance compared to factorized 2D self-attention, especially in spatiotemporal modeling.
Compared to 1D self-attention, the usage of 3D self-attention has demonstrated better performance, particularly in capturing both spatial and temporal relationships in the data.
Incorporating 3D self-attention in the finest feature maps (64 × 64) results in the highest fidelity images, although it leads to increased computational overhead during training and sampling.
To balance computational costs and fidelity, it was decided to use 3D self-attention only in feature maps of size 32 × 32 and smaller.
